event: Neolithic communities settle the coast and interior, herding livestock and fishing along the Arabian Gulf shoreline.
The site of Marawah Island sees some of the region's earliest permanent stone houses and early pearl-fishing activity.
The Umm an-Nar Bronze Age culture flourishes near Abu Dhabi, building distinctive circular communal tombs and trading with Mesopotamia.
Settlements at Hili in the Al Ain oasis develop sophisticated falaj irrigation and monumental round tombs.
The Wadi Suq culture emerges, marking a shift toward more mobile pastoralism and dispersed oasis settlement.
Iron Age communities introduce the underground falaj water channels that allow extensive date-palm agriculture in the interior.
The coastal trading town of Mleiha rises as a wealthy caravan and commerce center minting its own coinage.
The port of Ed-Dur on the Gulf coast thrives as a hub linking Roman, Mesopotamian, and Indian Ocean trade.
The region falls under the influence of the Sasanian Persian Empire, which dominates Gulf trade and politics.
The peoples of the region embrace Islam during the lifetime of the Prophet Muhammad, sending envoys to Medina.
The Battle of Dibba sees Rashidun caliphate forces suppress an apostasy revolt, cementing Islamic rule in the southeast.
Julfa (Julfar) near modern Ras Al Khaimah grows into a major Islamic-era port for Indian Ocean commerce.
Pearl diving and maritime trade flourish as Gulf ports connect the region to East Africa, India, and China.
Portuguese explorer Vasco da Gama's voyages open the era of European naval intrusion into the Gulf.
Portuguese forces under Afonso de Albuquerque seize and fortify coastal towns including Julfar to control Gulf trade.
The Portuguese are expelled from the lower Gulf as local powers and rival Europeans reassert control.
The Qawasim (Al Qasimi) maritime confederation rises as a dominant naval power along the coast around Ras Al Khaimah and Sharjah.
The Bani Yas tribe settles on Abu Dhabi island after fresh water is discovered, founding the future emirate's capital.
The Al Nahyan branch of the Bani Yas establishes its base in Abu Dhabi, beginning the ruling dynasty.
A British naval expedition attacks Qawasim ports, escalating conflict over Gulf shipping and trade.
Britain launches a major punitive campaign destroying Qawasim fleets and coastal strongholds at Ras Al Khaimah.
Local sheikhs sign the General Maritime Treaty with Britain, ending sanctioned piracy and giving the coast its 'Trucial' name.
A section of the Bani Yas breaks away under the Al Maktoum family and settles Dubai, founding that emirate.
The Treaty of Maritime Peace in Perpetuity binds the Trucial States to a permanent truce overseen by Britain.
The Exclusive Agreement makes the Trucial States a British protectorate, ceding foreign affairs to London.
Dubai's ruler grants tax exemptions to foreign merchants, beginning its rise as a free-trading commercial port.
The global Great Depression and the rise of Japanese cultured pearls collapse the region's vital pearling economy.
Sheikh Saeed bin Maktoum signs the first oil exploration concession for Dubai amid hopes of new wealth.
Britain forms the Trucial States Council, the first body uniting the seven sheikhdoms' rulers.
Commercially viable oil is discovered offshore at the Umm Shaif field near Abu Dhabi.
Abu Dhabi exports its first cargo of crude oil, launching the transformation of the region's economy.
Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan becomes ruler of Abu Dhabi and begins channeling oil revenue into development; Dubai also strikes oil.
Britain announces it will withdraw its forces from the Gulf by 1971, prompting talks on a federation of the sheikhdoms.
Six emirates form the United Arab Emirates on 2 December with Sheikh Zayed as president; Iran seizes the Tunbs and Abu Musa islands.
The UAE joins the Arab League and is admitted to the United Nations as a sovereign state.
Ras Al Khaimah joins the federation, completing the union of all seven emirates.
The UAE introduces the dirham as its single national currency, replacing the various Gulf rupees and riyals.
The UAE becomes a founding member of the Gulf Cooperation Council to coordinate regional security and economy.
Emirates airline is launched in Dubai, growing into one of the world's largest international carriers.
The UAE joins the international coalition against Iraq following the invasion of Kuwait.
A permanent constitution is adopted, with Abu Dhabi formally confirmed as the federal capital.
The landmark Burj Al Arab luxury hotel opens in Dubai, symbolizing the country's ambitious tourism push.
Founding president Sheikh Zayed dies and his son Sheikh Khalifa bin Zayed succeeds him as UAE president.
The first Federal National Council elections are held, introducing a limited form of representative consultation.
A Dubai debt crisis shakes global markets as the emirate seeks restructuring of its property-fueled borrowing.
The Burj Khalifa, the tallest building in the world, opens in Dubai.
The UAE joins the Saudi-led military intervention in Yemen's civil war.
The UAE signs the Abraham Accords normalizing relations with Israel and launches the Hope probe to Mars.
The Hope spacecraft enters Mars orbit and the UAE celebrates 50 years of union as it hosts the delayed Expo 2020 Dubai.
Sheikh Khalifa dies and Sheikh Mohamed bin Zayed Al Nahyan is elected the third president of the UAE.
The UAE hosts the COP28 United Nations climate summit in Dubai, brokering a global agreement to transition away from fossil fuels.
